titleEn: Pigs positioned as premier models for next-gen flu vaccines
titleZh: 猪被定位为下一代流感疫苗的首选模型
summaryZh: 本文报道了科学家利用猪作为研究流感病毒及其他呼吸道疾病（如冠状病毒和囊性纤维化）的理想动物模型。猪的肺部解剖结构、免疫反应和对流感病毒的易感性与人类高度相似，且作为“混合容器”在流感病毒跨物种传播中起关键作用。基因编辑、单细胞分析等技术的进步，使猪在开发下一代疫苗和疗法中价值凸显。该研究对猪业生物安全及人畜共患病防控具有重要启示。
translationZh:
随着世界持续应对不断演变的传染病，科学家们越来越多地将目光投向猪，以抗击流感。基因工程、免疫学和成像技术的进步，正使猪成为开发针对甲型流感病毒的下一代疫苗和疗法的最有前景的模型之一。

未来流感大流行的威胁依然严峻。全球人口增长、畜牧业的扩张以及人类与野生动物的接触增加，为新型流感病毒株的出现创造了理想条件。仅季节性流感每年就已导致大量疾病和死亡，同时给农业造成重大经济损失。尽管进行了数十年的研究，当前的疫苗和抗病毒药物提供的保护仍然有限。为了跟上病毒突变的速度，疫苗需要频繁更新，且往往无法阻止传播。

在《自然综述·免疫学》上发表的一篇文章中，一个包括皮尔布莱特研究所专家在内的国际研究团队，阐述了猪作为动物模型在受控条件下研究感染、操控免疫反应以及在人类使用前测试新疗法方面的作用。

**猪的肺部解剖结构、免疫反应以及对流感病毒的易感性与人类惊人地相似。** 猪是甲型流感病毒的自然宿主，病毒在猪体内的行为与在人类中非常相似。这使得基于猪的研究结果更有可能转化为有效的人类治疗方法。

技术进步显著增强了猪在研究中的实用性。科学家现在可以对猪进行基因改造，以更好地了解特定免疫细胞或蛋白质如何影响感染。单细胞分析领域的突破使科学家能够以前所未有的细节绘制猪的免疫系统图谱，揭示猪与人类免疫反应之间的相似性。皮尔布莱特研究所粘膜免疫学负责人、合著者埃尔玛·奇利安教授表示，猪是研究甲型流感病毒如何通过飞沫或受污染表面传播的绝佳模型。“这使得猪在测试旨在不仅治疗疾病而且阻止其传播的干预措施方面具有不可估量的价值。猪还可用于测试直接递送至呼吸道的新型疫苗和治疗策略，这些策略可能阻止传播并提供针对不同流感病毒的广泛保护。”

除了作为研究模型的作用外，猪在全球流感生态系统中也处于核心地位。全球约有15亿头猪，它们充当着人类、禽类和猪流感病毒株的“混合容器”。这为病毒交换基因创造了机会。
featuredReason: 该新闻揭示了猪作为人畜共患病研究模型的前沿价值，对智慧畜牧行业理解猪只健康管理、生物安全防控及跨学科技术融合具有前瞻性参考意义。
contentFull: Livestock ManagementHog HealthResearchPigs positioned as premier models for next-gen flu vaccinesScientists are already using swine to study other respiratory diseases, including coronaviruses and cystic fibrosis.The Pirbright InstituteJune 17, 2026schedule3 Min ReadNational Pork BoardAs the world continues to grapple with evolving infectious diseases, scientists are increasingly turning to pigs in the fight against influenza. Advances in genetic engineering, immunology and imaging technologies are positioning pigs as one of the most promising models for developing next generation vaccines and therapies against influenza A virus.The threat of a future flu pandemic remains significant. Global population growth, expanding livestock industries and increased human contact with wildlife have created ideal conditions for new strains of influenza to emerge. Seasonal flu alone already causes substantial illness and death each year, while also inflicting major economic losses on agriculture. Despite decades of research, current vaccines and antiviral drugs offer limited protection. Frequently updated to keep pace with viral mutations, they often fail to prevent transmission.Writing in Nature Reviews Immunology, a team of international researchers, including experts from The Pirbright Institute, explain the role of pigs as an animal model to study infections in controlled conditions, manipulate immune responses and test new treatments before they are used in humans.Related:Texas seeks FDA approval for feed-based ivermectinPig lung anatomy, immune responses and susceptibility to influenza viruses are remarkably similar to humans. Pigs are natural hosts for IAV and the virus behaves in them much as it does in humans. This makes findings from pig studies more likely to translate into effective human treatments.Technological advances have significantly enhanced the utility of pigs in research. Scientists can now genetically modify pigs to better understand how specific immune cells or proteins influence infection. Breakthroughs in single-cell analysis are allowing scientists to map the pig immune system in unprecedented detail, revealing similarities between pig and human immune responses. Professor Elma Tchilian, co-author and head of mucosal immunology at Pirbright, says that pigs are an excellent model to study how IAV spreads as droplets or via contaminates surfaces “This makes pigs invaluable for testing interventions designed not just to treat illness but to stop its spread. Pigs are also useful for testing novel vaccines and therapeutic strategies delivered directly to the respiratory tract which may prevent transmission and provide broad protection against different influenza viruses”Beyond their role as research models, pigs are also central to the global influenza ecosystem. With an estimated 1.5 billion pigs worldwide, they serve as ‘mixing vessels’ for human, avian and swine influenza strains. This creates opportunities for viruses to exchange genetic material and form new variants with pandemic potential.Related:Strengthen FAD preparedness: 3 essential steps for pork producersPigs are already used to study other respiratory diseases, including coronaviruses and cystic fibrosis. Their role in medical research continues to expand, highlighted by recent breakthroughs such as experimental pig-to-human organ transplants.Tchilian and her collaborators believe treatments and vaccines that prove effective in pigs are more likely to succeed in humans compared to those developed using traditional small-animal models. To fully unlock this potential, researchers emphasize the need for greater global collaboration. Standardizing experimental methods, sharing resources and building international research networks will be essential to accelerate progress.As the world seeks better ways to prepare for future pandemics, pigs may offer a crucial bridge between laboratory science and long-lasting defenses against influenza.About the AuthorThe Pirbright InstituteSee more from The Pirbright contentHtml: Scientists are already using swine to study other respiratory diseases, including coronaviruses and cystic fibrosis.
